title: Parallel & perpendicular lines from graph
description: Given points that two lines go through or the graph of each line, classify the lines as parallel, perpendicular, or neither.
topic_path: Parallel & perpendicular lines on the coordinate plane

assessment_items.question_content:
One line passes through the points $\blueD{(2,1)}$ and $\blueD{(5,7)}$.  Another line passes through points $\greenD{(-3,8)}$ and $\greenD{(8,3)}$.

**Are the lines parallel, perpendicular, or neither?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Slopes of parallel and perpendicular lines

Parallel lines have the same slope.

Perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals of each other.

###Step 1: Find the slope of the first line


>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{7-1}{5-2} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{6}{3} \\\\\
&=\blueD{2}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *increases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *positive* slope.

###Step 2: Find the slope of the second line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{3-8}{8-(-3)} \\\\\\
&= \greenD{\dfrac{-5}{11}}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *decreases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *negative* slope.

###Step 3: Comparing the slopes

The slopes are different, so the lines are not parallel.

The negative reciprocal of $2$ is $-\dfrac{1}{2}$, not $-\dfrac{5}{11}$, so the lines are not perpendicular.

###Answer

The lines are neither parallel nor perpendicular.



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
One line passes through the points $\blueD{(-7,-4)}$ and $\blueD{(5,4)}$.  Another line passes through points $\greenD{(-4,6)}$ and $\greenD{(6,-9)}$.

**Are the lines parallel, perpendicular, or neither?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Slopes of parallel and perpendicular lines

Parallel lines have the same slope.

Perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals of each other. 
###Step 1: Find the slope of the first line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{4-(-4)}{5-(-7)} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{8}{12} \\\\\\
&= \blueD{\dfrac{2}{3}}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *increases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *positive* slope.

###Step 2: Find the slope of the second line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{-9-6}{6-(-4)} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{-15}{10} \\\\\
&= \greenD{-\dfrac{3}{2}}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *decreases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *negative* slope.
###Step 3: Comparing the slopes

The negative reciprocal of $\blueD{\dfrac{2}{3}}$ is $\greenD{-\dfrac{3}{2}}$, so the lines are perpendicular.
###Answer

The lines are perpendicular.



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
One line passes through the points $\blueD{(-6,0)}$ and $\blueD{(-4,6)}$.  Another line passes through points $\green{(5,-2)}$ and $\green{(8,7)}$.

**Are the lines parallel, perpendicular, or neither?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Slopes of parallel and perpendicular lines

Parallel lines have the same slope.

Perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals of each other.

###Step 1: Finding the slope of the first line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{6-0}{-4-(-6)} \\\\\\
&={\dfrac{6}{2}} \\\\\\
&= \blueD{3}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *increases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *positive* slope.

###Step 2: Finding the slope of the second line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{7-(-2)}{8-5} \\\\\\
&= {\dfrac{9}{3}} \\\\\\
&= \greenD{3}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *increases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *positive* slope.

###Step 3: Comparing the slopes

The slopes are equal, so the lines are parallel.

###Answer

The lines are parallel.



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
One line passes through the points $\blueD{(-3,-1)}$ and $\blueD{(2,4)}$.  Another line passes through points $\greenD{(2,-6)}$ and $\greenD{(7,-1)}$.

**Are the lines parallel, perpendicular, or neither?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Slopes of parallel and perpendicular lines

Parallel lines have the same slope.

Perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals of each other.

###Step 1: Finding the slope of the first line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{4-(-1)}{2-(-3)} \\\\\\
&= {\dfrac{5}{5}} \\\\\
&= \blueD{1}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *increases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *positive* slope.

###Step 2: Finding the slope of the second line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{-1-(-6)}{7-2} \\\\\\
&= {\dfrac{5}{5}} \\\\\
&= \greenD{1}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *increases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *positive* slope.

###Step 3: Comparing the slopes

The slopes are equal, so the lines are parallel.

###Answer

The lines are parallel.



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
One line passes through the points $\blueD{(-7,5)}$ and $\blueD{(-1,-3)}$.  Another line passes through points $\greenD{(0,3)}$ and $\greenD{(3,-1)}$.

**Are the lines parallel, perpendicular, or neither?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Slopes of parallel and perpendicular lines

Parallel lines have the same slope.

Perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals of each other.

###Step 1: Finding the slope of the first line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{-3-5}{-1-(-7)} \\\\\\
&= {\dfrac{-8}{6}} \\\\\\
&=\blueD{-\dfrac{4}{3}}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *decreases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *negative* slope.

###Step 2: Finding the slope of the second line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{-1-3}{3-0} \\\\\\
&= \greenD{\dfrac{-4}{3}}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *decreases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *negative* slope.

###Step 3: Comparing the slopes

The slopes are equal, so the lines are parallel.

###Answer

The lines are parallel.



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
One line passes through the points $\blueD{(-8,1)}$ and $\blueD{(4,4)}$.  Another line passes through points $\greenD{(-9,-7)}$ and $\greenD{(9,-3)}$.

**Are the lines parallel, perpendicular, or neither?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Slopes of parallel and perpendicular lines

Parallel lines have the same slope.

Perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals of each other.

###Step 1: Finding the slope of the first line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{4-1}{4-(-8)} \\\\\\
&= {\dfrac{3}{12}} \\\\\\
&= \blueD{\dfrac{1}{4}}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *increases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *positive* slope.

###Step 2: Finding the slope of the second line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{-3-(-7)}{9-(-9)} \\\\\\
&= {\dfrac{4}{18}} \\\\\\
&= \greenD{\dfrac{2}{9}}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *increases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *positive* slope.

###Step 3: Comparing the slopes

The slopes are different, so the lines are **not** parallel.

The negative reciprocal of $\blueD{\dfrac{1}{4}}$ is $-4$, not $\greenD{\dfrac{2}{9}}$, so the lines are **not** perpendicular.

###Answer

The lines are neither parallel nor perpendicular.



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
One line passes through the points $\blueD{(1,9)}$ and $\blueD{(4,-9)}$.  Another line passes through points $\greenD{(6,5)}$ and $\greenD{(8,-5)}$.

**Are the lines parallel, perpendicular, or neither?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Slopes of parallel and perpendicular lines

Parallel lines have the same slope.

Perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals of each other.

###Step 1: Finding the slope of the first line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{-9-9}{4-1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{-18}{3} \\\\\\
&= \blueD{-6}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *decreases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *negative* slope.

###Step 2: Finding the slope of the second line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{-5-5}{8-6} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{-10}{2} \\\\\\
&= \greenD{-5}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *decreases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *negative* slope.

###Step 3: Comparing the slopes

The slopes are different, so the lines are **not** parallel.

The negative reciprocal of $\blueD{-6}$ is $\dfrac{1}{6}$, not $\greenD{-5}$, so the lines are **not** perpendicular.

###Answer

The lines are neither parallel nor perpendicular.



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
One line passes through the points $\blueD{(-3,-1)}$ and $\blueD{(1,-9)}$.  Another line passes through points $\greenD{(1,4)}$ and $\greenD{(5,6)}$.

**Are the lines parallel, perpendicular, or neither?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Slopes of parallel and perpendicular lines

Parallel lines have the same slope.

Perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals of each other.

###Step 1: Finding the slope of the first line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{-9-(-1)}{1-(-3)} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{-8}{4} \\\\\\
&= \blueD{-2}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *decreases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *negative* slope.

###Step 2: Finding the slope of the second line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{6-4}{5-1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{2}{4} \\\\\\
&= \greenD{\dfrac{1}{2}}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *increases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *positive* slope.

###Step 3: Comparing the slopes

The negative reciprocal of $\blueD{-2}$ is $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$, so the lines are perpendicular.

###Answer

The lines are perpendicular.



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
One line passes through the points $\blueD{(-7,4)}$ and $\blueD{(5,-4)}$.  Another line passes through points $\greenD{(-7,-4)}$ and $\greenD{(2,2)}$.

**Are the lines parallel, perpendicular, or neither?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[Not convincing visually like most of the almost perpendicular lines, but addresses the common misconception that perpendicular lines have opposite slopes.]:
hints:
###Slopes of parallel and perpendicular lines

Parallel lines have the same slope.

Perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals of each other.

###Step 1: Finding the slope of the first line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{-4-4}{5-(-7)} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{-8}{12} \\\\\\
&= \blueD{-\dfrac{2}{3}}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *decreases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *negative* slope.

###Step 2: Finding the slope of the second line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{2-(-4)}{2-(-7)} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{6}{9} \\\\\\
&= \greenD{\dfrac{2}{3}}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *increases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *positive* slope.

###Step 3: Comparing the slopes

The slopes are different, so the lines are **not** parallel.

The negative reciprocal of $\blueD{-\dfrac{2}{3}}$ is $\dfrac{3}{2}$, not $\greenD{\dfrac{2}3{}}$, so the lines are **not** perpendicular.

###Answer

The lines are neither parallel nor perpendicular.



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
One line passes through the points $\blueD{(-2,1)}$ and $\blueD{(4,9)}$.  Another line passes through points $\greenD{(-3,8)}$ and $\greenD{(5,2)}$.

**Are the lines parallel, perpendicular, or neither?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[Mixing up points yields parallel lines]:
hints:
###Slopes of parallel and perpendicular lines

Parallel lines have the same slope.

Perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals of each other.

###Step 1: Finding the slope of the first line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{9-1}{4-(-2)} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{8}{6} \\\\\\
&= \blueD{\dfrac{4}{3}}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *increases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *positive* slope.

###Step 2: Finding the slope of the second line

>$\begin{align} 
\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} &= \dfrac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{2-8}{5-(-3)} \\\\\\
&= \dfrac{-6}{8} \\\\\\
&= \greenD{-\dfrac{3}{4}}
\end{align}$

The $y$-value *decreases* as the $x$-value increases, so it makes sense that this line has a *negative* slope.

###Step 3: Comparing the slopes

The negative reciprocal of $\blueD{\dfrac{4}{3}}$ is $\greenD{-\dfrac{3}{4}}$, so the lines are perpendicular.

###Answer

The lines are perpendicular.



[[☃ image 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
**Are the lines in the figure parallel, perpendicular, or neither?**

[[☃ radio 1]]



[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
### The lines clearly aren't perpendicular, but are they parallel?

Parallel lines have the same slope, so if we find the slope of each line, we can tell if the lines are parallel.

###Step 1: Finding the slope of each line

Slope is the ratio of the vertical and horizontal changes between any two points on a line.

The slope of $\blueD{\text{line }a}$:

> $\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = {\dfrac{-4}{9}}$

The slope of $\greenD{\text{line }b}$:

> $\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = {\dfrac{-4}{9}} $



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Step 2: Comparing the slopes

The slopes are equal, so the lines are parallel.

###Answer

The lines are parallel.

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes both scale by one.Two lines are on the plane. Line a decreases from left to right and passes through points negative six, seven and three, three. Line b decreases from left to right and passes through points negative four, one and five, negative three.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Are the lines in the figure parallel, perpendicular, or neither?**

[[☃ radio 1]]



[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
###The lines clearly aren't parallel, but are they perpendicular?

Perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals of each other.

###Step 1: Finding the slope of each line

Slope is the ratio of the vertical and horizontal changes between any two points on a line.

The slope of $\blueD{\text{line }a}$:

> $\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = {\dfrac{12}{4}}=\blueD{3}$

The slope of $\greenD{\text{line }b}$:

> $\dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = {\dfrac{-3}{9}} =\greenD{-\dfrac{1}{3}}$

###Step 2: Comparing the slopes

The negative reciprocal of $\blueD{3}$ is $\greenD{-\dfrac{1}{3}}$, so the lines are perpendicular.

###Answer

The lines are perpendicular.
